article_categories.name: Research summaries
article_categories.description: Plain-language summaries of recent peer-reviewed research on non-invasive brain stimulation.

glossary_terms.definition: tDCS configuration where the anode (positively charged electrode) is placed over the target region, typically associated with increased excitability.
glossary_terms.details: In conventional tDCS, anodal stimulation is thought to depolarize neuronal membranes slightly, making neurons more likely to fire. For example, placing the anode over the motor cortex (M1) at 1–2 mA for 10–20 minutes can transiently enhance motor cortex excitability. Effects depend on montage, current density, session duration, and individual variability. Anodal stimulation does not always enhance performance and may interact with task difficulty and baseline performance.

glossary_terms.definition: tDCS configuration where the cathode (negatively charged electrode) is placed over the target region, often associated with reduced excitability.
glossary_terms.details: Cathodal stimulation has historically been associated with slight hyperpolarization of neuronal membranes, making neurons less likely to fire. For example, cathodal tDCS over motor cortex at 1–2 mA can reduce corticomotor excitability. However, effects are not universally inhibitory and may reverse at higher currents or different montages. DIY users should be cautious about assuming simple 'on/off' effects and should prioritize safety over performance enhancement.

glossary_terms.definition: Amount of electrical current per unit electrode area, often expressed in mA/cm².
glossary_terms.details: Current density is a critical safety parameter in tDCS and other direct current techniques. It is calculated by dividing the applied current (in mA) by the contact area of the electrode (in cm²). Higher current densities increase the risk of skin irritation or burns. Many human tDCS studies use current densities below about 0.05–0.08 mA/cm². DIY builders should compute current density for each montage and avoid exceeding values used in peer-reviewed research.

glossary_terms.definition: A non-invasive brain stimulation technique that applies weak direct current through scalp electrodes to modulate cortical excitability.
glossary_terms.details: tDCS uses low-amplitude direct current, typically 0.5–2 mA, delivered for 10–30 minutes via sponge or rubber electrodes. Unlike TMS, tDCS does not directly evoke action potentials but biases neuronal firing probability. DIY interest has grown due to relatively simple hardware; however, risk management, electrode preparation, and conservative parameter choices are essential. Users should consult safety guidelines, avoid unsupervised experimentation in vulnerable populations, and recognize the limited predictability of cognitive effects.

glossary_terms.definition: A non-invasive stimulation method delivering sinusoidal or other alternating currents through the scalp to interact with brain oscillations.
glossary_terms.details: tACS typically uses currents similar in amplitude to tDCS (around 0.5–2 mA) but oscillating at specific frequencies (e.g., alpha at 10 Hz, gamma at 40 Hz). It is used in research to probe or modulate brain rhythms. Compared to tDCS, safety considerations additionally involve perception of phosphenes and potential entrainment of rhythms. DIY use is more complex because artifact-free monitoring is difficult and optimal parameters are less established.

glossary_terms.definition: A non-invasive technique that uses rapidly changing magnetic fields to induce electric currents in the brain, directly triggering neuronal firing.
glossary_terms.details: TMS requires specialized equipment and is not suited for typical DIY setups. It can deliver single pulses, repetitive trains, or patterned protocols and is used clinically for depression and other conditions. Compared with tDCS or tACS, TMS produces stronger, more focal effects, but also carries different risk profiles, including rare seizure risk. Articles comparing tDCS, tACS, and TMS often highlight differences in focality, mechanism, and regulatory status.

glossary_terms.definition: A frontal brain region often targeted in stimulation studies of mood, working memory, and executive function.
glossary_terms.details: In tDCS and TMS research, the left or right DLPFC is a common target for protocols related to depression, cognitive control, and decision-making. DIY montages often approximate DLPFC using landmarks such as F3 or F4 from the 10–20 EEG system, but placement accuracy can vary. When following any DLPFC protocol example, users should avoid clinical self-treatment and should follow conservative safety parameters for current and duration.

glossary_terms.definition: Primary motor cortex, a common tDCS target for experiments on motor learning and excitability.
glossary_terms.details: The motor cortex is often identified around C3 or C4 in the 10–20 system, with precise localization refined using TMS or neuronavigation in research settings. DIY users sometimes approximate M1 using simple measurements from anatomical landmarks. Protocols may use 1–2 mA for 10–20 minutes. Safety checklists for M1-focused tDCS often emphasize skin preparation, avoiding excessive current density, and monitoring for persistent tingling or headaches.

glossary_terms.definition: The specific arrangement and placement of electrodes on the head and body during a stimulation session.
glossary_terms.details: A montage is typically described by the positions of anode and cathode (for tDCS) or multiple electrodes (for tACS and HD-tDCS), often using the 10–20 EEG system. For example, an "anode over left DLPFC (F3), cathode over right supraorbital" montage is common in some studies. Safety and efficacy both depend on montage, as current flow patterns change with electrode locations, size, and contact quality.

glossary_terms.definition: A conductive interface between the stimulation device and the skin, usually made of metal and surrounded by saline-soaked sponges or gel.
glossary_terms.details: Electrodes for tDCS and tACS are often rubber or sintered metal, used with sponges or gel to distribute current and reduce skin irritation. Key parameters include size, material, and how well they maintain contact without excessive pressure. DIY users should avoid bare metal on dry skin, ensure even contact, and regularly inspect the skin under each electrode for redness or burning sensations.

glossary_terms.definition: A control condition designed to mimic the sensations of stimulation without delivering a full dose.
glossary_terms.details: In sham tDCS, the device may ramp current up for 20–30 seconds and then ramp down to zero, creating initial tingling without sustained stimulation. This helps blind participants in research studies. Some DIY devices implement a sham mode to help users evaluate placebo effects and expectation. Sham conditions are not risk-free and still require basic safety checks.

glossary_terms.definition: Redness, itching, or burning sensations on the skin under or around electrodes during or after stimulation.
glossary_terms.details: Mild, transient redness is common in tDCS and tACS, but sharp pain, burning, or persistent marks may indicate excessive current density, poor contact, or allergic reactions. Strategies to reduce irritation include using larger electrodes, adequate saline, avoiding broken skin, and ensuring no metal edges press into the skin. Users should stop sessions if strong pain occurs and avoid re-stimulating irritated areas.

glossary_terms.definition: A common but usually mild side effect reported after non-invasive brain stimulation sessions.
glossary_terms.details: Headaches can arise from muscle tension, anxiety, or the stimulation itself. They are typically short-lived and mild, but persistent or severe headaches warrant stopping DIY use and, if needed, consulting a clinician. Adjusting strap tension, reducing current, shortening sessions, and ensuring good hydration can help. Articles on side effects often discuss how to monitor and log headache severity over time.

glossary_terms.definition: The brain's ability to change its structure and function in response to experience, learning, or stimulation.
glossary_terms.details: Many brain stimulation techniques aim to harness neuroplasticity, either to enhance learning or support recovery after injury. Repeated tDCS or tACS sessions may interact with natural plasticity mechanisms, but long-term effects in healthy DIY users are not fully understood. Ethical guidelines typically recommend caution with frequent, long-lasting protocols without clear medical supervision.

glossary_terms.definition: The strength of electrical current delivered during a session, usually measured in milliamperes (mA).
glossary_terms.details: Common human tDCS protocols use intensities between 0.5 and 2 mA. Higher intensities increase skin sensation and potential risk. For DIY use, many safety guides suggest starting at the lower end of this range, using gradual ramp-up and ramp-down, and avoiding multiple high-intensity sessions per day. Intensity always needs to be interpreted together with electrode size and session duration.

glossary_terms.definition: The total length of time that current is applied during a stimulation session.
glossary_terms.details: Session duration in tDCS research is often between 10 and 30 minutes. Longer durations may produce stronger or more lasting after-effects but can also increase side-effect risk. DIY users planning 15–20 minute sessions should pay attention to cumulative time across multiple daily sessions, keep a log, and monitor for increasing side effects such as headaches or sleep changes.

tools.name: Montage visual helper
tools.description: Interactive diagrams to explore common electrode montages over motor cortex, DLPFC, and other targets.

example_protocols.name: Left DLPFC focus: 1 mA for 15–20 minutes (beginner template)
example_protocols.description: Beginner-friendly tDCS example placing the anode over left DLPFC (F3) and the cathode over right supraorbital area. Uses 5×5 cm sponge electrodes and emphasizes conservative current density and careful skin checks before and after the 15–20 minute session.

example_protocols.name: High-dose left DLPFC protocol: 2 mA for 20–30 minutes
example_protocols.description: Advanced protocol example for experienced users, illustrating a 2 mA, 20–30 minute montage over left DLPFC with extracephalic return. Includes warnings about increased side-effect risk and the importance of medical supervision for repeated use.

example_protocols.name: M1 anodal stimulation: 1.5 mA for 20 minutes
example_protocols.description: Example protocol matching many research-style motor learning designs: anode over contralateral M1 (C3/C4), cathode over the opposite supraorbital area, 1.5 mA with 30-second ramp up/down and a fixed 20-minute plateau.

example_protocols.name: Short-session M1 tDCS: 1 mA for 10–15 minutes
example_protocols.description: Conservative motor cortex example for newcomers focusing on short sessions. Uses larger electrodes to keep current density low and includes guidance on stopping early if strong tingling or headaches appear.

example_protocols.name: Occipital alpha tACS: 1 mA at 10 Hz for 10–20 minutes
example_protocols.description: Illustrative tACS protocol delivering 10 Hz (alpha-band) stimulation over occipital cortex with bilateral return electrodes. Highlights phosphene sensations, eye safety, and the need for dark-room testing in research settings.

example_protocols.name: Frontal gamma tACS: 1 mA at 40 Hz for 15–20 minutes
example_protocols.description: Protocol example showing 40 Hz tACS with bilateral frontal electrodes to illustrate how stimulation frequency and montage can be combined. Includes notes on avoiding late-evening sessions due to possible sleep disruption.

example_protocols.name: Left DLPFC rTMS-style train: 10 Hz blocks
example_protocols.description: Educational rTMS example describing 10 Hz trains over left DLPFC at typical clinical intensities (e.g., 110–120% of resting motor threshold). Provided for comparison only; not suitable for DIY implementation.

example_protocols.name: HD-tDCS 4×1 ring over M1: 2 mA for 20 minutes
example_protocols.description: High-definition tDCS example using a central anode over M1 with four surrounding cathodes. Demonstrates more focal current delivery compared with large sponge electrodes and warns about careful electrode preparation to avoid hotspots.

example_protocols.name: Prefrontal transcranial random noise stimulation (tRNS): 1 mA for 20 minutes
example_protocols.description: Example of tRNS parameters with broadband random noise over bilateral prefrontal areas. Used to illustrate how timing and intensity compare with standard tDCS, with extra emphasis on conservative use and documentation of any unusual sensations.

example_protocols.name: Cerebellar tDCS: 1.5 mA for 15–20 minutes
example_protocols.description: Protocol example positioning the anode over the right cerebellar hemisphere with a contralateral buccinator return. Included to demonstrate non-cortical targets and additional neck/strap comfort considerations.

learning_path_templates.name: 4-week DIY brain stimulation basics
learning_path_templates.description: A four-week introduction to non-invasive brain stimulation concepts, devices, and key terminology for new DIY readers.

learning_path_templates.name: Safety, side effects, and ethics (3 weeks)
learning_path_templates.description: Short path that focuses on practical safety checklists, common side effects like skin irritation and headaches, and core ethical considerations.

learning_path_templates.name: Intermediate DIY tDCS device building
learning_path_templates.description: Five-week track covering component selection, layout, parts lists, and validation steps for DIY tDCS hardware builders.

learning_path_templates.name: Protocols and electrode placement fundamentals
learning_path_templates.description: A focused path on reading and interpreting protocol examples, understanding DLPFC and motor cortex montages, and logging parameters safely.

diy_parts.name: 9V battery clip with leads
diy_parts.notes: Standard snap connector to attach a single 9V battery to the circuit input.

diy_parts.name: 9V alkaline battery
diy_parts.notes: Fresh, non-rechargeable 9V battery recommended for stable voltage during short beginner sessions.

diy_parts.name: DC-DC step-down (buck) module
diy_parts.notes: Used to regulate the supply voltage to a stable level before current limiting.

diy_parts.name: Fixed resistor 1 kΩ, 1/4 W
diy_parts.notes: Provides a hard current limit in series with the output to reduce risk of accidental overcurrent.

diy_parts.name: Potentiometer 10 kΩ (panel mount)
diy_parts.notes: Allows fine adjustment of output current within a safe range when combined with the fixed resistor.

diy_parts.name: LED power indicator (3 mm)
diy_parts.notes: Shows when the device is powered; include a suitable series resistor if not on a module.

diy_parts.name: SPST toggle switch
diy_parts.notes: Main on/off power switch placed between the battery and the rest of the circuit.

diy_parts.name: 2 x 3.5 mm panel-mount jacks for electrodes
diy_parts.notes: Provides detachable connections for anode and cathode electrode cables.

diy_parts.name: ABS plastic enclosure (approx. 120×70×40 mm)
diy_parts.notes: Non-conductive box to house the circuit and protect the user from exposed wiring.

diy_parts.name: Knob for potentiometer
diy_parts.notes: Improves precision and comfort when adjusting the output setting.

diy_parts.name: Dual 9V battery holders with leads
diy_parts.notes: Allows a higher supply voltage for wider compliance range when using current regulation ICs.

diy_parts.name: Linear current regulator IC (e.g., LM334 or similar)
diy_parts.notes: Forms the core of a constant-current source; must be configured within safe current limits.

diy_parts.name: Multi-turn trimmer resistor 2 kΩ
diy_parts.notes: Used to calibrate the current regulator precisely to target values like 1 mA or 2 mA.

diy_parts.name: Panel-mount rotary switch (3 positions)
diy_parts.notes: Lets the user select between preset current levels (e.g., 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 mA).

diy_parts.name: Miniature panel voltmeter module (3–30 V)
diy_parts.notes: Displays output voltage or battery voltage; helps with quick safety sanity checks.

diy_parts.name: Miniature panel ammeter module (0–20 mA)
diy_parts.notes: Shows the actual output current delivered to the load or test resistor.

diy_parts.name: Inline fuse holder
diy_parts.notes: Holds a fast-acting fuse in series with the supply to protect against wiring faults.

diy_parts.name: Fast-acting fuse 100 mA
diy_parts.notes: Limits maximum fault current; one used in the circuit, one spare.

diy_parts.name: 4 x panel-mount 3.5 mm jacks for electrodes and test loads
diy_parts.notes: Two jacks dedicated to electrodes, two reserved for connecting a test resistor during calibration.

diy_parts.name: Shielded electrode extension cables
diy_parts.notes: Reduces noise pickup and adds strain relief when connecting to sponge electrodes.

diy_parts.name: Larger ABS project enclosure with mounting hardware
diy_parts.notes: Provides space for meters, switches, batteries, and internal wiring with safe clearances.

diy_parts.name: Rubber grommets and strain relief bushings
diy_parts.notes: Protects cables where they exit the enclosure to prevent fraying and accidental disconnection.

diy_build_parts_lists.title: Simple current-limited tDCS build (beginner-friendly parts list)
diy_build_parts_lists.description: Compact parts list for a basic 9V battery-powered tDCS device with passive current limiting, a single control knob, and simple electrode jacks. Designed to keep the component count low while still including a hard current-limiting resistor and an enclosure for safety.

diy_build_parts_lists.title: Advanced metered tDCS build (intermediate parts list)
diy_build_parts_lists.description: More complex parts list for an intermediate DIY tDCS device that uses a dedicated current regulator IC, includes panel meters for voltage and current, and adds safety features like fuses and strain relief. Intended for builders comfortable with soldering and calibration.

diy_build_parts_lists.title: USB-powered minimal tDCS prototype
diy_build_parts_lists.description: Experimental minimal parts list for a USB-powered tDCS prototype that emphasizes low current and clear separation between the USB ground and user electrodes. Not recommended as a first build and provided mainly for discussion of design trade-offs.

diy_build_parts_lists.title: Dual-channel tACS experimental rig
diy_build_parts_lists.description: High-complexity parts list for a dual-channel tACS rig, including separate amplitude controls, shielding considerations, and optional monitoring outputs. Intended for advanced experimenters comparing tDCS and tACS hardware designs.

comments.body: This comparison was really helpful. I didn't realize how different the mechanisms of tDCS and TMS actually are. For a cautious DIYer mostly interested in mild modulation, it sounds like sticking with low-intensity tDCS is the more realistic option?

Yes, for DIY users we strongly recommend staying within conservative tDCS parameters that are in line with published human studies, and avoiding any attempt to replicate clinical TMS hardware. This article is intended to clarify differences, not to encourage unsupervised clinical-style use.
Could you add a quick table summarizing typical intensities and session durations for tDCS vs tACS? It would be nice to see the usual 1–2 mA / 10–20 minute ranges side by side.
Good suggestion. We just added a comparison table under the "Typical research parameters" heading, including common ranges for current intensity, session duration, and target areas.
I appreciate the section on regulatory status. The reminder that none of this is a substitute for medical care is important, especially for people thinking about mood or attention issues.

For someone comparing tDCS and tACS specifically for working memory experiments at home, would you say the evidence base is still stronger for tDCS? I’m not planning anything intense, just short, well-spaced sessions.

I sometimes get a dull headache after a 20-minute, 1.5 mA motor cortex session. I’m using 5×5 cm sponge electrodes. Would dropping to 1 mA or shortening to 15 minutes be a reasonable first step to see if it helps?

Common first adjustments include reducing current (for example from 1.5 mA to 1.0 mA), shortening session duration, and adding longer ramp-up/ramp-down periods. Also check strap tension, hydration, and sleep. If headaches persist or worsen, stop DIY sessions and discuss with a clinician.
The skin care tips here were useful. Switching to fresh saline each time and making sure the sponges are evenly soaked reduced both itching and post-session headaches for me.

Thanks for keeping this protocol conservative. I used the 1 mA / 10-minute version with large electrodes and mostly felt mild tingling. No headaches so far. I like that you emphasize logging each session and skipping days when anything feels off.

Glad it was helpful. For newcomers, staying at or below 1 mA with shorter 10–15 minute sessions and careful electrode preparation is usually a safer starting point than jumping directly to longer or stronger protocols.

For the 1 mA, 20-minute left DLPFC example, do you recommend starting with fewer minutes at first (like 10–15) to see how someone reacts, or is it okay to begin with the full 20 minutes if they have no relevant medical history?

We generally suggest a gradual approach: shorter test sessions first, careful monitoring for side effects, and avoiding frequent or back-to-back sessions. Even when parameters match published research, responses vary across individuals.

The simple build guide was easy to follow. I like that the parts list includes a hard current-limiting resistor even though there’s also a potentiometer. Do you think adding a small inline fuse would noticeably improve safety for beginners?

An inline fuse sized below any plausible fault current is a good extra safeguard, especially if someone might modify the circuit later. It won’t replace careful design and testing, but it can help limit the consequences of wiring mistakes.
The calibration section was great. Using a test resistor before ever connecting electrodes made me much more confident that the current regulator was actually limited to 1–2 mA.

articles.title: 1 mA, 20-minute tDCS protocol over left DLPFC (example)
articles.summary: Step-by-step example protocol for a conservative 1 mA, 15–20 minute anodal tDCS session targeting the left DLPFC, with emphasis on safety and logging.
articles.content: This article walks through a conservative tDCS protocol targeting the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) using 1 mA for 15–20 minutes. We describe approximate electrode placement using the 10–20 EEG system (anode over F3, cathode over the right supraorbital area), electrode size assumptions for safe current density, and ramp-up/ramp-down procedures. A worked example protocol is included under the "Example protocol" section, along with checklists for pre-session screening, skin inspection, and post-session logging. This content is informational only and is not a substitute for medical care.

articles.title: Advanced DLPFC tDCS variants: montages and higher intensities
articles.summary: Overview of higher-intensity and alternative-montage DLPFC tDCS protocols for experienced readers, highlighting added risks and uncertainties.
articles.content: Building on the basic DLPFC example protocol, this article surveys a range of more complex tDCS setups used in research, including 2 mA sessions, extracephalic return montages, and multi-session designs. For each variant, we discuss the rationale, typical electrode positions, and how current density compares with more conservative beginner templates. We repeatedly stress that pushing beyond 1–1.5 mA and 20 minutes increases uncertainty and potential risk. This discussion is aimed at helping readers interpret research papers—not at encouraging unsupervised high-dose self-experimentation.

articles.title: Motor cortex (M1) tDCS: 1.5 mA for 20 minutes
articles.summary: Worked example of a 1.5 mA, 20-minute anodal M1 tDCS protocol with detailed montage diagrams and safety notes.
articles.content: This protocol article outlines a 1.5 mA, 20-minute anodal tDCS session over primary motor cortex (M1). We describe how to approximate C3 or C4 using the 10–20 system, how to place the return electrode supraorbitally, and how to calculate approximate current density from electrode size. The article explains ramping, recording of sensations, and how to adjust parameters downward if side effects such as headaches appear. It also contrasts this setup with shorter beginner-oriented M1 protocols covered elsewhere on the site.

articles.title: Beginner M1 tDCS protocol: short 1 mA sessions
articles.summary: A conservative, beginner-focused M1 tDCS protocol with 1 mA and 10–15 minute sessions, plus guidance on logging and when to stop.
articles.content: This beginner-focused protocol keeps parameters conservative: 1 mA current, 10–15 minute session duration, and relatively large sponge electrodes to keep current density low. We outline step-by-step preparation, including checking for skin issues, positioning electrodes over M1 and supraorbital regions, and using a gradual ramp-up. The article includes suggestions for maintaining a session log, spacing sessions over days, and stopping immediately if sharp pain, strong tingling, or worrisome headaches occur. It is intended purely as an educational template based on commonly reported research parameters.

articles.title: Occipital alpha tACS protocol: 10 Hz visual cortex example
articles.summary: An advanced example of 10 Hz tACS over occipital cortex, focusing on visual phosphenes, comfort, and research-style precautions.
articles.content: In this advanced guide we present an example protocol for 1 mA, 10 Hz tACS over occipital cortex. We describe common electrode montages, expected visual phosphenes, and why many researchers conduct sessions in darkened rooms. The article emphasizes that tACS is more complex to characterize than tDCS, especially without EEG monitoring, and is not recommended as a starting point for DIY experimenters. Parameter tables and notes on ramping are provided for educational comparison.

articles.title: Frontal gamma tACS: 40 Hz bilateral DLPFC example
articles.summary: Example parameters for 40 Hz gamma-band tACS over bilateral DLPFC, with cautions about sleep and cognitive side effects.
articles.content: This protocol-style overview describes a 1 mA, 40 Hz tACS montage targeting bilateral DLPFC. We discuss approximate electrode positions, strategies to minimize skin irritation, and the limited evidence base for cognitive enhancement claims. The article cautions against evening sessions due to possible sleep disruption and recommends that DIY users avoid frequent or long-term gamma tACS without clinical oversight.

articles.title: rTMS over DLPFC: clinical-style protocol overview (for comparison only)
articles.summary: Educational overview of rTMS protocols over DLPFC used in clinical settings, provided to contrast with DIY-suitable methods.
articles.content: This article explains, in general terms, how repetitive TMS (rTMS) protocols over the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex are structured in clinical practice. We review typical frequencies, train durations, and total pulse counts, and compare them to low-intensity tDCS and tACS approaches. The article clearly states that TMS hardware is not appropriate for DIY replication and that rTMS should only be administered in regulated clinical environments. A short example layout is included purely for conceptual comparison.

articles.title: HD-tDCS 4×1 ring montage over M1: focused stimulation example
articles.summary: A focused HD-tDCS protocol example using a 4×1 ring arrangement over M1, with detailed discussion of electrode preparation.
articles.content: Here we describe a 4×1 ring HD-tDCS configuration centered over primary motor cortex. The article covers electrode layout, current distribution considerations, and the increased risk of hotspots if small electrodes are not prepared carefully. We contrast this setup with large-pad M1 montages and point readers to simulation tools often used in research. Due to its complexity, this material is aimed at experienced experimenters, not first-time builders.

articles.title: Prefrontal tRNS: exploratory 1 mA, 20-minute example
articles.summary: An exploratory overview of transcranial random noise stimulation (tRNS) parameters over prefrontal cortex.
articles.content: This protocol-inspired article introduces transcranial random noise stimulation (tRNS) with a 1 mA, 20-minute example over bilateral prefrontal regions. We explain how tRNS differs from tDCS and tACS, describe broad-spectrum and high-frequency variants, and summarize what small research studies have reported so far. Because the parameter space is less well established, we emphasize caution and suggest that most DIY readers focus on better-understood tDCS safety frameworks instead.

articles.title: Cerebellar tDCS example protocol for coordination experiments
articles.summary: Example parameters for cerebellar tDCS at 1.5 mA for 15–20 minutes, aimed at understanding coordination and balance tasks.
articles.content: In this article we discuss an example cerebellar tDCS setup using 1.5 mA for 15–20 minutes, based loosely on published motor learning studies. We cover electrode placement over the right cerebellar hemisphere, return electrode location, and extra comfort considerations around the neck and jaw. The article also highlights that cerebellar targets may affect balance and coordination, so readers should avoid situations where a fall would be dangerous after stimulation.

articles.title: Simple current-limited DIY tDCS build guide (beginner)
articles.summary: A beginner-friendly DIY tDCS build with a hard current-limiting resistor, single control knob, and clear safety notes.
articles.content: This build guide shows how to assemble a simple 9 V battery-powered tDCS device using a fixed series resistor, a potentiometer for fine tuning, and a small number of off-the-shelf parts. Each step stresses insulating exposed conductors, double-checking polarity before use, and testing the output into a dummy resistor load before ever connecting electrodes to skin. The guide is aimed at readers who are new to electronics and includes photos, wiring diagrams, and a compact parts list.

articles.title: Intermediate DIY tDCS build guide: metered constant-current design
articles.summary: An intermediate DIY tDCS build using a current-regulator IC and panel meters to monitor voltage and current.
articles.content: This guide targets builders comfortable with soldering and basic circuit debugging. It walks through a constant-current tDCS design based on a dedicated regulator IC, complete with calibration steps using a fixed resistor load and panel meters. Safety-focused sections explain why a fuse is included, how to verify that current never exceeds 2 mA, and how to label controls clearly. A detailed parts list is provided along with enclosure layout suggestions and strain relief tips.

articles.title: USB-powered DIY tDCS build guide: minimal prototype
articles.summary: A minimal, experimental USB-powered tDCS prototype guide for intermediate builders, with strong cautions about isolation and grounding.
articles.content: This article explores a minimal tDCS prototype that draws power from a USB source. It emphasizes the importance of galvanic isolation, careful grounding decisions, and conservative current limits. The design is intentionally simple so readers can compare it against battery-powered builds and understand trade-offs. Due to additional safety considerations around mains-connected equipment, this guide is not recommended as a first project.

articles.title: Advanced dual-channel tACS DIY build guide
articles.summary: An advanced build guide for a dual-channel tACS rig, covering amplitude control, shielding, and monitoring outputs.
articles.content: This advanced build centers on a dual-channel tACS device capable of driving two independent montages. We discuss oscillator design, amplitude control, channel isolation, and the importance of using proper shielding and grounding to reduce interference. The article includes warnings about unintended current paths and recommends that only experienced experimenters with test equipment attempt this project. A comprehensive parts list and mechanical layout suggestions are included.

articles.title: Comparing tDCS, tACS, and TMS: mechanisms, safety, and DIY relevance
articles.summary: A detailed comparison of tDCS, tACS, and TMS covering mechanisms, typical parameters, safety profiles, and what reasonably fits within DIY boundaries.
articles.content: This overview article explains how tDCS, tACS, and TMS differ in terms of mechanism, focality, intensity, and regulatory status. We provide a side-by-side table of typical research parameters, including current or field strengths and session durations, and discuss which techniques are realistically compatible with conservative DIY use. The article repeatedly stresses that TMS hardware should not be built or used outside regulated clinical settings and that even tDCS and tACS require careful adherence to safety guidelines.

articles.title: Side effects spotlight: headaches and skin irritation with DIY tDCS
articles.summary: Beginner-friendly overview of common tDCS side effects such as headaches and skin irritation, with practical tips for reducing risk.
articles.content: This article reviews some of the most commonly reported side effects of tDCS: mild headaches, tingling, itching, and temporary skin redness. We explain which sensations are typical and when they may indicate that you should stop a session immediately. Practical tips include using larger electrodes to reduce current density, checking strap tension, adjusting current and session duration, and keeping a log of symptoms. The article also highlights red-flag symptoms that warrant discontinuing DIY use and consulting a clinician.

articles.title: DIY brain stimulation basics: safety first with home tDCS
articles.summary: A quick-start safety overview for beginners covering the basics of safe home tDCS use.
articles.content: This short guide introduces the basics of safe home tDCS practice for newcomers. It outlines core safety principles: staying within conservative current and duration ranges, checking for medical contraindications, avoiding unsupervised use for self-treatment, and keeping detailed session notes. The article links to more in-depth resources on electrode preparation, side effects, and ethics, and is designed to be readable in under ten minutes.

articles.title: tDCS electrode placement basics: a beginner's safety guide
articles.summary: Beginner-level explanation of electrode placement basics for tDCS, including simple montages and safety checks.
articles.content: This article explains electrode placement basics for tDCS using clear diagrams and everyday language. We introduce the 10–20 system at a high level, show how common montages place electrodes over motor cortex or DLPFC, and discuss why electrode size and contact quality matter for safety. Readers are encouraged to start with well-known, conservative montages rather than improvising placements. A short checklist at the end summarizes key steps to follow before each session.

articles.title: Short guide to tDCS side effects and headaches: safety checklist
articles.summary: A concise checklist-style overview of tDCS side effects, focusing on headaches and what to do when they appear.
articles.content: This short article is structured as a checklist to help beginners recognize and respond to common tDCS side effects, especially headaches. We cover pre-session screening questions, in-session warning signs, and post-session red flags. Simple adjustments—such as reducing current, shortening sessions, improving electrode preparation, and spacing sessions further apart—are described. Readers are reminded that persistent or severe headaches are a reason to stop DIY use and seek professional advice.

articles.title: Ethics of DIY brain stimulation: side effects, consent, and limits
articles.summary: Beginner-oriented ethics overview covering informed consent, side-effect uncertainty, and reasonable limits on DIY brain stimulation.
articles.content: This article introduces key ethical questions around DIY brain stimulation. We discuss why informed consent still matters in self-experimentation, how uncertainty about long-term side effects should shape conservative parameter choices, and why self-treatment for serious conditions is risky without medical supervision. The article encourages readers to define clear stop conditions, respect others’ boundaries, and keep experiments modest in intensity and duration. It also links to the site-wide disclaimer and research summaries for further context.
